name: Void
subtitle: Midnight · Hours 0 to 3
versicle:
You are alone with the dark now. The house settles; the field lies still.

Nothing is asked of you.
phenomena: Orion transits the meridian
Dew begins to form on grass blades
Screech-owl calls from the wood edge

quotes.text: In the middle of the journey of our life I came to myself within a dark wood where the straight way was lost.
Darkness is not the absence of light; it is the presence of the night.
quotes.attr: Clark Strand, Waking Up to the Dark

quotes.text: The most beautiful thing we can experience is the mysterious. It is the source of all true art and science.

images.caption: Louisa S. Cook, Geometrical Psychology, or, The Science of Representation: An Abstract of the Theories and Diagrams of B. W. Betts (1887)

name: Silence
subtitle: Pre-dawn · Hours 3 to 6
versicle: Something shifts. The darkness has not changed, but there is a loosening at its edge — not light yet, but the memory of light.
phenomena: Body temperature reaches its nadir
Flying squirrel glides between oaks
First vague pallor at the east horizon

quotes.text: How often have I lain beneath rain on a strange roof, thinking of home.
It is a beauteous evening, calm and free; the holy time is quiet as a nun breathless with adoration.
Before the birds have begun, before the day has made any claim, the soul is most itself.

name: Chorus
subtitle: Dawn · Hours 6 to 9
versicle:
The robin sings first, always the robin — Turdus migratorius, the wandering thrush — before the light has committed to anything.

Then the wood thrush. Then the world remembers it is permitted to begin.
phenomena: American Robin sings first: 45 min before sunrise
Eastern Bluebird on the fence post
Spiders string dew on grass blades

quotes.text: Then the sun rose up from a bed of vapors, and seemed fairly to dissolve with tenderness and warmth. For an hour or two the air was perfectly motionless, and full of low, humming, awakening sounds. The naked trees had a rapt, expectant look. From some unreclaimed common near by came the first strain of the song sparrow; so homely, because so old and familiar, yet so inexpressibly pleasing. Presently a full chorus of voices arose, tender, musical, half suppressed, but full of genuine hilarity and joy. The bluebird warbled, the robin called, the snowbird chattered, the meadowlark uttered her strong but tender note. Over a deserted field a turkey buzzard hovered low, and alighted on a stake in the fence, standing a moment with outstretched, vibrating wings till he was sure of his hold. A soft, warm, brooding day. Roads becoming dry in many places, and looking so good after the mud and the snow. I walk up beyond the boundary and over Meridian Hill. To move along the drying road and feel the delicious warmth is enough. The cattle low long and loud, and look wistfully into the distance. I sympathize with them. Never a spring comes but I have an almost irresistible desire to depart. Some nomadic or migrating instinct or reminiscence stirs within me. I ache to be off.
Early in the morning, when the world is young, the sun coming up from behind the hills — that is the time to see all things new.
The light comes in sideways, the way grace is said to come — not from above, but through the cracks at the edge of things.

images.caption: Limbourg brothers, Anatomical Zodiac Man (or L'Homme anatomique), from Les Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry (c. 1412–1416)
Dawn chorus; the robin's extravagant claim on the morning

name: Transit
subtitle: Morning · Hours 9 to 12
versicle: Light is now something you move through rather than look at. The day is underway, and you are in it.
phenomena: Monarch butterfly nectaring at milkweed
Bumblebee queen foraging for nest material
Photosynthesis at full rate in the canopy

quotes.text:
I have heard what the talkers were talking, the talk of the beginning and the end,
But I do not talk of the beginning or the end.

There was never any more inception than there is now,
Nor any more youth or age than there is now,
And will never be any more perfection than there is now,
Nor any more heaven or hell than there is now.

Urge and urge and urge,
Always the procreant urge of the world.
quotes.attr: Walt Whitman, from Song of Myself (1892)

quotes.text:
We shall not cease from exploration
And the end of all our exploring
Will be to arrive where we started
And know the place for the first time.
Through the unknown, remembered gate
When the last of earth left to discover
Is that which was the beginning;
At the source of the longest river
The voice of the hidden waterfall
And the children in the apple-tree
Not known, because not looked for
But heard, half-heard in the stillness
Between two waves of the sea.
quotes.attr: T. S. Eliot, from "Little Gidding" (1942), Four Quartets (1943)

images.caption: Charles Darwin, from The Power of Movement in Plants (1880)

name: Fulcrum
subtitle: Noon · Hours 12 to 15
versicle:
The sun stands at the top of its arc. The shadow is directly beneath you.

For a moment, there is no pointing away.
phenomena: Solar zenith; shadows at minimum length
Cicada chorus at peak intensity
Linnaean Horologium Florae: chicory closes its petals

quotes.text: All really inhabited space bears the essence of the notion of home.

The climax of the drama is the place in the piece where the results of the rising movement come out strong and decisively; it is almost always the crowning point of a great, amplified scence, enclosed by the smaller connecting scenes of the rising, and of the falling action. The poet needs to use all the splendor of poetry, all the dramatic skill of his art, in order to make vividly conspicuous the middle point of his artistic creation. [...] This outburst of deed from the soul of the hero, or the influx of portentous impressions into the soul; the first great result of a sublime struggle, or the beginning of a mortal inward conflict,--must appear inseperably connected with what goes before as well as with what follows; it will be brought into relief through broad treatment or strong effect; but it will, as a rule, be represented in its development from the rising movement and its effect on the environment; therefore, the climax naturally forms the middle point of a group of forces, which, darting in either direction, course upward and downward.
quotes.attr: Gustav Freytag, Technique of the Drama (1894)

quotes.text: Nature is not a place to visit. It is home.

images.caption: Hilma af Klint, Group IX/UW, No. 25, The Dove, No. 1 (1915)

name: Doldrums
subtitle: Afternoon · Hours 15 to 18
versicle: The angle of the light has become philosophical. Every object casts a shadow longer than itself.
phenomena: Goldenrod lit at the field edge
Monarch forming waypoint roost
Evening primrose opens its first flowers

quotes.text: Adopt the pace of nature: her secret is patience.
Time is the longest distance between two places.
The vesper sparrow gives its name to this hour, and to a particular quality of late-day song that sounds like something ending and not minding.

images.caption: Claude Monet, Stacks of Wheat (End of Summer) (1890-1891)

name: Repast
subtitle: Evening · Hours 18 to 21
versicle:
The light lingers until it is gone.

This is the hour of the lamp and the window. From outside, the lit rooms look like devotion.
phenomena: Common Nighthawk begins aerial foraging
First fireflies flash in open fields
Peepers at maximum volume in the wetland chorus

quotes.text: There is something infinitely healing in the repeated refrains of nature.
The sky holds blue after the sun has gone. Ornithologists call this civil twilight, as if to say: we permit this.
Evening is the time when the heart is most open.

images.caption: Solis occasus; the light's formal withdrawal
Crepusculum; the blue that stays when the light has gone

name: Denouement
subtitle: Night · Hours 21 to 24
versicle:
The old monks said this was the close of the day: completorium, the completing.

Something may be laid down now.
phenomena: Milky Way transiting; core visible from dark sites
Great Horned Owl calls from the pine stand
Orion rising at the east horizon

quotes.text:
The bleak fields are asleep,
My heart alone wakes;
The evening in the harbour
Down his red sails takes.


Night, guardian of dreams,
Now wanders through the land;
The moon, a lily white,
Blossoms within her hand.

Steep with deep sweetness,
O You White Magnolias,
This still torpid night!
quotes.attr: Richard Wright,#9 (c. 1959) Haiku: This Other World (1998)

quotes.text: The dawn! The sun! The light! Haw! The long blue days, for his head, for his side, and the little paths for his feet, and all the brightness to touch and gather. Through the grass the little mosspaths, bony with old roots, and the trees sticking up, and the flowers sticking up, and the fruit hanging down, and the white exhausted butterflies, and the birds never the same darting all day into hiding. And all the sounds, meaning nothing. Then at night rest in the quiet house, there are no roads, no streets any more, you lie down by the window opening on refuge, the little sounds come that demand nothing, ordain nothing, explain nothing, propound nothing, and the short necesssary night is soon ended, and the sky blue again over all the secret places where nobody ever comes, the secret places never the same, but always simple and indifferent, always mere places, sites of a stirring beyond coming and going, of a being so light and free that it is as the being of nothing.

images.caption: Arkhip Kuindzhi, Moonlit Night on the Dnieper (1880)
Agnes Giberne, from The Story of the Sun, Moon, and Stars (1898)
